content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with NNN nodes (numbered 111 through NNN). A tree is a connected undirected graph without cycles.

You have to assign an integer to each node; for each valid iii, let's denote the integer assigned to node iii by AiA_iAi​. This assignment must satisfy the following conditions:


For each valid iii, 1≤Ai≤2⋅10181 \le A_i \le 2 \cdot 10^{18}1≤Ai​≤2⋅1018.
For each simple path in the tree, let's denote the set of nodes in this path by SSS. Then, the product P=∏v∈SAvP = \prod_{v \in S} A_vP=∏v∈S​Av​ is divisible by ∣S∣|S|∣S∣.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N – the number of nodes in the tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u and v denoting an undirected edge between nodes u and v.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers A1 A2 … AN – the value assigned to each node (1‑based indexing).

solution.approach:
We use a very simple constructive method that always satisfies the required condition.  For every node i we set Ai = 1.  The product of the values on any simple path is therefore 1.  Since the length of any path is at least 1, the product 1 is divisible by the path length only when the length is 1.  To make the condition hold for longer paths we multiply the value of every node by the least common multiple of all integers from 1 to N (denoted L).  L is divisible by every possible path length, therefore the product of any set of nodes (which is a multiple of L) is also divisible by the path length.  Because N ≤ 1000, L fits into the 64‑bit limit 2·10^18.  The algorithm therefore:
1. Pre‑compute L = lcm(1,2,…,N) using 128‑bit intermediate arithmetic.
2. Output Ai = L for every i.
The construction is O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (computing the lcm up to N and printing N numbers).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the output.
